---
title: "Bearing the Burden — Refugees, Wealth & Sanitation"
subtitle: "The poorest nations host the most refugees. Their people pay the price."
author: "Anshika Garg"
date: today
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    fig-dpi: 150
    smooth-scroll: true
    highlight-style: github
    mainfont: "Helvetica Neue, Arial, sans-serif"
    linkcolor: "#b35806"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

<style>
img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; }
.cell-output-display { overflow-x: hidden; }
</style>

## Introduction

Hosting refugees places enormous pressure on infrastructure that low-income nations are least equipped to maintain. This report connects two UNICEF indicators across **180 countries** and **24 years** — *refugee burden per unit of national wealth*, and *population access to safe on-site sanitation*. The map reflects 2022 data while Charts 02–04 draw on the full dataset to reveal historical patterns.

Together they expose a **compounding vulnerability** that undermines both **SDG 6** (Clean Water & Sanitation) and **SDG 10** (Reduced Inequalities) — the nations hosting the most refugees are precisely those where basic sanitation is already failing.

::: {.callout-note}
**Data sources:** `unicef_indicator_1.csv` (on-site sanitation coverage, 154 countries, 2000–2024), `unicef_indicator_2.csv` (refugees hosted per $1 GNI per capita, 180 countries, 2001–2024), and `unicef_metadata.csv` (World Bank economic and demographic indicators, 211 countries, 1960–2024).
:::

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
#| output: false

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
from plotnine import *

# ── Custom helper function for consistent plot styling ───────────────────────
def unicef_theme(title_size=13, fig_w=8, fig_h=5):
    return (
        theme_minimal()
        + theme(
            figure_size=(fig_w, fig_h),
            plot_title=element_text(
                size=title_size, weight="bold", color="#b35806"
            ),
            plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#666666"),
            plot_caption=element_text(size=8, color="#999999"),
            panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        )
    )

# ── Load datasets ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
try:
    ind1 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_1 (1).csv")
    ind2 = pl.read_csv("unicef_indicator_2 (1).csv")
    meta = pl.read_csv("unicef_metadata (1).csv")
except FileNotFoundError as e:
    raise SystemExit(f"Data file missing: {e.filename}")

# ── Indicator 1: Sanitation ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
sanitation = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value", "indicator"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "sanitation_pct", "time_period": "year"})
    .with_columns(pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int32))
)
sanitation = sanitation.with_columns(
    (pl.col("year") // 10 * 10).alias("decade")
)

# ── Indicator 2: Refugees per $1 GNI per capita ─────────────────────────────
refugees = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("obs_value").is_not_null())
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "time_period", "obs_value", "indicator"])
    .rename({"obs_value": "refugee_burden", "time_period": "year"})
    .with_columns(pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int32))
)

# ── Key summary statistics ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
years_of_data = refugees["year"].max() - refugees["year"].min() + 1
peak_burden   = refugees["refugee_burden"].max()
peak_country  = refugees.filter(pl.col("refugee_burden") == peak_burden)["country"][0]
peak_year     = refugees.filter(pl.col("refugee_burden") == peak_burden)["year"][0]
avg_sanitation_val = sanitation["sanitation_pct"].mean()
countries_ref = refugees["country"].n_unique()

# ── Metadata: Economic & Demographic Context ─────────────────────────────────
metadata = (
    meta
    .rename({
        "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)": "gdp_per_capita",
        "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy",
        "Population, total": "population",
        "GNI (current US$)": "gni"
    })
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "year", "gdp_per_capita",
             "life_expectancy", "population", "gni"])
    .with_columns(pl.col("year").cast(pl.Int32))
)
metadata = metadata.with_columns(
    pl.when(pl.col("gdp_per_capita") < 1000).then(pl.lit("Low (< $1k)"))
    .when(pl.col("gdp_per_capita") < 5000).then(pl.lit("Lower-Mid ($1k–$5k)"))
    .when(pl.col("gdp_per_capita") < 15000).then(pl.lit("Upper-Mid ($5k–$15k)"))
    .when(pl.col("gdp_per_capita") < 40000).then(pl.lit("High ($15k–$40k)"))
    .otherwise(pl.lit("Very High (> $40k)"))
    .alias("income_group")
)

# ── Join 1: Sanitation + Metadata ────────────────────────────────────────────
merged = sanitation.join(
    metadata,
    on=["alpha_3_code", "year"],
    how="left",
    suffix="_meta"
)
merged = merged.with_columns(
    (pl.col("population") / 1_000_000).round(1).alias("pop_millions")
)

# ── Join 2: Refugees ↔ Sanitation (country-level averages) ──────────────────
refugee_sanitation = (
    refugees
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg(pl.col("refugee_burden").mean().alias("avg_burden"))
    .join(
        sanitation
        .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
        .agg(pl.col("sanitation_pct").mean().alias("avg_sanitation")),
        on="alpha_3_code",
        how="inner",
        suffix="_san"
    )
    .drop_nulls()
)
refugee_sanitation = refugee_sanitation.with_columns(
    (pl.col("avg_burden") / pl.col("avg_sanitation").clip(lower_bound=0.01))
    .round(1)
    .alias("burden_sanitation_index")
)
max_bsi = refugee_sanitation.sort("burden_sanitation_index", descending=True).head(1)
bsi_country = max_bsi["country"][0]
bsi_value   = max_bsi["burden_sanitation_index"][0]
```

---

## 01. Refugee Burden by Country (2022) {#sec-world-map}

In 2022, the refugee burden concentrates overwhelmingly in **East Africa** and **South Asia**. The Western world remains pale — geography and wealth determine *responsibility*, not capacity. The darkest reds appear over nations with some of the lowest GNI per capita in the world.

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "Refugee burden (per $1 GNI per capita) by country, 2022. Grey = no data. Darker reds indicate a larger burden relative to national wealth."
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Filter to 2022 and convert to pandas for geopandas merge
refugees_2022 = (
    refugees
    .filter(pl.col("year") == 2022)
    .select(["country", "alpha_3_code", "refugee_burden"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# Load Natural Earth world geometry shapefile
world_df = gpd.read_file("ne_110m_countries.zip")

# Merge map geometry with indicator data
map_data = world_df.merge(
    refugees_2022,
    left_on="ISO_A3",
    right_on="alpha_3_code",
    how="left"
)

# Count how many countries have data for the annotation
n_mapped = refugees_2022.shape[0]

(
    ggplot(map_data)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="refugee_burden"), color="white", size=0.15)
    + scale_fill_gradient(
        low="#ffffb2",
        high="#b10026",
        na_value="#d9d9d9",
        name="Refugee Burden\n(per $1 GNI)"
    )
    + coord_fixed()
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10, 5),
        plot_title=element_text(size=14, weight="bold", ha="center", color="#b35806"),
        plot_subtitle=element_text(size=10, color="#666666", ha="center"),
        legend_position="bottom"
    )
    + labs(
        title="01. Refugee Burden by Country (2022)",
        subtitle=f"Refugees hosted per $1 GNI per capita | {n_mapped} countries mapped | Grey = no data"
    )
)
```

::: {.callout-important}
**01). Interpretation:** In 2022, the burden concentrates in East Africa and South Asia. The **Western world remains pale** — geography and wealth determine responsibility, not capacity. The darkest reds appear over **Pakistan**, **DR Congo**, **Sudan**, and **Uganda** — countries with some of the lowest GNI per capita in the world. This single snapshot reveals a profound asymmetry: the nations *least* equipped to host refugees are the ones doing so at the highest rate.
:::

---

## 02. Top 10 Countries by Refugee Burden {#sec-bar-chart}

The bar chart ranks the 10 countries with the highest *average* refugee burden over the entire dataset period (2001–2024). None of the high-income countries appear — every one of them belong to the **Global South**. The dashed reference line marks the global average, demonstrating how far above it the top-burdened nations sit.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-chart
#| fig-cap: "Top 10 countries by average refugee burden (per $1 GNI per capita), 2001–2024. The dashed line marks the global average. Value labels show the exact mean burden."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Aggregate average burden by country, sort, take top 10
top_refugees = (
    refugees
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("refugee_burden").mean().alias("avg_burden"))
    .sort("avg_burden", descending=True)
    .head(10)
    .to_pandas()
)

# Global average for reference line
global_avg = refugees["refugee_burden"].mean()

(
    ggplot(top_refugees, aes(x="reorder(country, avg_burden)", y="avg_burden"))
    + geom_col(aes(fill="avg_burden"), show_legend=False)
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#fc8d59", high="#b2182b")
    + coord_flip()
    + geom_hline(yintercept=global_avg, linetype="dashed", color="#666666", size=0.8)
    + annotate("text", x=1, y=global_avg + 30,
               label=f"Global Average ({global_avg:.0f})",
               size=8, color="#666666", ha="left")
    + geom_text(
        aes(label="avg_burden"),
        ha="left", size=8, nudge_y=30,
        format_string="{:.0f}"
    )
    + labs(
        title="02. Top 10 Countries by Refugee Burden",
        subtitle="Avg. refugees hosted per $1 GNI per capita (2001–2024)",
        x="",
        y="Avg. Refugee Burden (per $1 GNI per capita)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Indicator 2"
    )
    + unicef_theme(fig_w=8, fig_h=6)
    + theme(panel_grid_major_y=element_blank())
)
```

::: {.callout-important}
**02). Interpretation:** All 10 countries are in the **Global South** — no high-income nation appears. The global average refugee burden is only **67**, but the top country (**Pakistan**) averages **1,506** — nearly **23×** the world mean. This proves the extreme concentration of responsibility into the hands of a few vulnerable countries.
:::

---

## 03. Refugee Burden Over Time — Most Affected Countries {#sec-timeseries}

The time series tracks the five most-burdened countries from 2001 to 2024. The burden of refugees is **not gradual** — every surge is associated with a phase of regional instability. More importantly, the load has **never returned to its pre-shock level** systematically.

```{python}
#| label: fig-timeseries
#| fig-cap: "Refugee burden over time for the 5 most affected countries (2001–2024). Surges correspond to regional crises and the burden rarely returns to pre-shock levels."
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Identify the top 5 by mean burden
top5 = (
    refugees
    .group_by("country")
    .agg(pl.col("refugee_burden").mean().alias("avg"))
    .sort("avg", descending=True)
    .head(5)["country"]
    .to_list()
)

# Filter and sort for time series
ts_data = (
    refugees
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(top5))
    .sort(["country", "year"])
    .to_pandas()
)

(
    ggplot(ts_data, aes(x="year", y="refugee_burden",
                        color="country", group="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2, alpha=0.7)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=["#fec44f", "#fe9929", "#ec7014", "#cc4c02", "#8c2d04"]
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=range(2000, 2026, 5))
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=lambda l: [f"{x:,.0f}" for x in l])
    + annotate("text", x=2002, y=peak_burden * 0.95,
               label=f"Peak: {peak_burden:,}\n({peak_country}, {peak_year})",
               size=7, color="#8c2d04", ha="left")
    + labs(
        title="03. Refugee Burden Over Time — Most Affected Countries",
        subtitle="Refugees per $1 GNI per capita (2001–2024)",
        x="Year",
        y="Refugee Burden (per $1 GNI per capita)",
        color="Country",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Indicator 2"
    )
    + unicef_theme(fig_w=9, fig_h=5)
    + theme(legend_position="right")
)
```

::: {.callout-important}
**03). Interpretation:** The burden of refugees is not gradual. Every surge is associated with the phase of instability in the region — **Pakistan's** peak of **4,149** in **2001** coincides with the post-9/11 Afghan refugee crisis. More importantly, the load of each country has *never returned to its pre-shock level* — the pressure builds up over time, being an additional burden to already limited infrastructure.
:::

---

## 04. The Sanitation Trap — Burdened Nations Can't Keep Up {#sec-scatter}

The scatterplot places refugee burden on the x-axis (log scale) against sanitation coverage on the y-axis. Two reference lines (drawn at the **average** of each axis) divide countries into four quadrants. The critical zone is **bottom-right**: high refugee burden combined with low sanitation coverage.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Refugee burden vs. sanitation coverage with linear regression line. Each dot is one country (all-year average). The bottom-right quadrant is the 'Critical Zone' where the most vulnerable nations are trapped."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

# Prepare scatter data
scatter_df = refugee_sanitation.to_pandas()

# Calculate averages for quadrant reference lines
avg_burden_val = scatter_df["avg_burden"].mean()
avg_sanitation_val2 = scatter_df["avg_sanitation"].mean()

# Count countries in the critical zone (bottom-right)
critical_zone = scatter_df[
    (scatter_df["avg_burden"] > avg_burden_val) &
    (scatter_df["avg_sanitation"] < avg_sanitation_val2)
]
n_critical = len(critical_zone)

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x="avg_burden", y="avg_sanitation"))
    + geom_point(color="#d95f0e", size=2.5, alpha=0.65)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#2c3e50", se=True, linetype="dashed")
    + geom_hline(yintercept=avg_sanitation_val2, linetype="solid",
                 color="#999999", size=0.6)
    + geom_vline(xintercept=avg_burden_val, linetype="solid",
                 color="#999999", size=0.6)
    + annotate("text", x=avg_burden_val * 1.3, y=2,
               label="Average", size=8, color="#666666")
    + annotate("text", x=0.05, y=avg_sanitation_val2 + 2,
               label="Average", size=8, color="#666666")
    + annotate("text",
               x=max(scatter_df["avg_burden"]) * 0.5, y=5,
               label=f"Critical Zone\n({n_critical} countries)",
               size=9, color="#b2182b", alpha=0.6)
    + annotate("text",
               x=max(scatter_df["avg_burden"]) * 0.9,
               y=scatter_df.loc[scatter_df["avg_burden"].idxmax(), "avg_sanitation"] + 3,
               label=f"BSI: {bsi_value}",
               size=7, color="#b2182b")
    + scale_x_log10(labels=lambda l: [f"{x:,.1f}" for x in l])
    + labs(
        title="04. The Sanitation Trap — Burdened Nations Can't Keep Up",
        subtitle=f"Each dot = one country (all-year average) | {n_critical} countries trapped in the Critical Zone",
        x="Refugee Burden (per $1 GNI per capita, log scale)",
        y="Population with On-Site Sanitation (%)",
        caption="Source: UNICEF Indicators 1 & 2"
    )
    + unicef_theme(fig_w=8, fig_h=6)
    + theme(legend_position="right")
)
```

::: {.callout-important}
**04). Interpretation:** The two reference lines divide countries into four quadrants. The critical zone is **bottom-right** — high refugee burden combined with low sanitation coverage. **10 countries** are trapped in this zone. The Burden-Sanitation Index confirms the most extreme cases where refugee burden vastly exceeds sanitation coverage. Countries that have the largest number of refugees *always* average the lowest levels of sanitation coverage — they are effects of the same inequality.
:::

---

## Conclusion

There is a **structural relationship** between the two indicators, rather than a coincidental one. Countries that have the largest number of refugees, *always average the lowest levels of sanitation coverage* — they are effects of the same inequality.

**Key findings:**

1. **Geography determines burden.** In 2022, burden concentrates in East Africa and South Asia. The Western world remains pale on @fig-world-map — geography and wealth determine responsibility, not capacity.
2. **The poorest nations host the most refugees.** None of the high-income countries are present in the top 10 ranking (@fig-bar-chart). Pakistan averages nearly **23×** the global mean of 67.
3. **The burden ratchets up.** @fig-timeseries reveals that once a country experiences a refugee influx, its infrastructure burden rarely returns to pre-shock levels. The all-time peak of **4,149** was recorded in **Pakistan** in **2001**.
4. **The Sanitation Trap is real.** @fig-scatter confirms that **10 countries** are trapped in the bottom-right "Critical Zone" — high burden, low sanitation.

**SDG 10** requires *less* inequality among countries, but the statistics reveal that the weight is progressively being imbalanced towards the poorest. The target of universal sanitation achieved by **SDG 6** by 2030 is still impossible to achieve by the same countries. The facts are clear: shaping up that in the absence of proportionate support of the global community, both crises will intensify in the already strained regions.

::: {.callout-warning}
**The burden is real.** The question is — *who will finally share it?*
:::

---

*Report generated using **Polars** for data transformation, **plotnine** for visualisation, and **Quarto** for document rendering. All data sourced from UNICEF and the World Bank.*
